Hakusan Hime Shrine |- |21 || {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|lt=Hachiōji Gongen|lt=Hachioji Gongen|de|Hachioji-Gongen|de|Hachioji-Gongen|ja|八王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Gongen|qq=}} || Hiyoshi Taisha Hachioji Shrine |- |22 || Inari Great Deity || [[:en:Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] |- |23 || Sumiyoshi Great Deity || [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] |- |24 || Gion Zen-in|1=Sekizan Zen-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269244}} |- |26 || Tatebe Great Deity || [[:en:Takebe taisha|Tatebe Taisha]] |- |27 || Mikami Great Deity || [[:en:Mikami Shrine|Mikami Shrine]] |- |28 || Hyoshuda Great Deity || {{ill|Hyoshuda Taisha|de|Hyoshuda-Taisha|de|Hyoshuda-Taisha|ja|兵主大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyoshuda Taisha|1=Hyozu-taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392795}} |- |29 || Nawabuka Great Deity || {{ill|Nawabuka Shrine|de|Nawabuka-Schrein|de|Nawabuka-Schrein|ja|那波加神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawabuka Shrine|1=Nahaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642755}} |- |30 || Kibitsu Great Deity || [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] |} The Sanjūban-shin were established on the premise of a state safeguarded by Buddhist teachings as recorded in various scriptures – foremost among them the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]], as well as texts such as the Vajra Transmission Sutra and [[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 毘沙門天|1=Vaiśravaṇa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q866315}}, [[:en:Dhṛtarāṣṭra|Jikokuten]], [[:en:Hariti|Kishimojin]], and [[:en:Ten Rākṣasīs|Ten Rakshasa Women]].[http|//www.hokkeshu.com/event/dic_sa_30.html 法華宗(陣門流)｜三十番神、三十一日はどうなるの] == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}} {{Shinto stub}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Medieval Shinto}} [[:en:Ja|三十番神]] [[:en:de|Dreißig Schutzgötter]] [[:en:Sn|Sanju Banshin]] [[:en:Ja|%E4%B8%89%E5%8D%81%E7%95%AA%E7%A5%9E]] {{draft categories| [[:en:Category|Shinto-Buddhism Syncretism]] [[:en:Category|Lotus Buddhism]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Deities]] [[:en:Category|Dharma Guardians]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Thirty Deities]] [[:en:Category|Completion tier 1]] [[:en:Category|11]] }} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ja:三十番神]] [[Category:Added translation template]]{{translated page|ja|三十番神|version=102345351|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 30]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Nichiren Buddhism]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] {{wikidata link|Q11354495}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 毘沙門天|1=Vaiśravaṇa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q866315}}, [[:en:Dhṛtarāṣṭra|Jikokuten]], [[:en:Hariti|Kishimojin]], and [[:en:Ten Rākṣasīs|Ten Rakshasa Women]].[http|//www.hokkeshu.com/event/dic_sa_30.html 法華宗(陣門流)｜三十番神、三十一日はどうなるの] == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}} {{Shinto stub}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Medieval Shinto}} [[:en:Ja|三十番神]] [[:en:de|Dreißig Schutzgötter]] [[:en:Sn|Sanju Banshin]] [[:en:Ja|%E4%B8%89%E5%8D%81%E7%95%AA%E7%A5%9E]] {{draft categories| [[:en:Category|Shinto-Buddhism Syncretism]] [[:en:Category|Lotus Buddhism]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Deities]] [[:en:Category|Dharma Guardians]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Thirty Deities]] [[:en:Category|Completion tier 1]] [[:en:Category|11]] }} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ja:三十番神]] [[Category:Added translation template]]{{translated page|ja|三十番神|version=102345351|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 30]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Nichiren Buddhism]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] {{wikidata link|Q11354495}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11354495}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11354495]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sanjūban-shin''' (三十番神) are a group of thirty deities of the Shinto-Buddhism syncretic faith who were believed to protect the state and its people on a daily rotating basis. The Sanjūban-shin guard the thirty days of a month (since the old lunisolar calendar, [[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]], had either 29 or 30 days) in rotation. It is said that the founder of [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]], [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] (posthumously honored as Dengyō Daishi), enshrined them on [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] – this being the origin of their veneration. Their worship flourished during the Kamakura period. From the medieval era onward they were especially emphasized within [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren Buddhism]] and {{ill|Hokke Buddhism|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|ja|法華宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Buddhism|1=Hokke-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q44733349}} (in the context of {{ill|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|ja|法華神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Shintō|1=Hokke Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555328}}), being regarded as deities safeguarding the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]] – specifically as [[:en:Dharmapala|Various Benevolent Deities]]. This incorporation was undertaken by [[:en:Nichizō|Nisshō]], who, in his efforts to propagate Nichiren Buddhism in Kyoto, introduced the Mount Hiei Sanjūban-shin into his missionary work. In addition, [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shintō]] presented an alternative grouping of Sanjūban-shin – proclaiming, for instance, the “Guardians of Heaven and Earth,” “Guardians of the Royal Castle,” and “Guardians of the Nation.” [[:en:Yoshida Kanetomo|Yoshida Kanku]] argued that the Sanjūban-shin worship originated from Yoshida Shintō. In Yoshida Shintō there are also accounts linking them with the “Thirty‐two Deities” from the time of the [[:en:Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson]].{{Cite journal|和書|author=宮川了篤 |url=https|//doi.org/10.15054/00000290 |title=平成二十二年一月二十七日 最終講義 日蓮宗修法史概説 (宮川了篤先生退職記念号) |journal=身延論叢 |publisher=身延山大学仏教学会 |year=2011 |issue=16 |pages=15-16 |ISSN=1342-2715 |doi=10.15054/00000290}} 『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1224160/26 国民思想叢書. 神道篇]』　加藤咄堂 編　国民思想叢書刊行会 「神道大意」p.23-24（国立国会図書館） In 1868, with the advent of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]], the enshrinement of these deities was temporarily prohibited in the early Meiji period (although they are now freely enshrined in various forms – such as in inner sanctuary altars or subsidiary shrines – at the discretion of individual sects and temples). There are traditionally considered to be about ten different types of Sanjūban-shin, namely the Guardians of Heaven and Earth, Guardians of the Palace, Guardians of the Royal Castle, Guardians of the Nation, Guardians of the Imperial Court, Guardians of the Lotus, Guardians of the True Law, Guardians of the Lotus Sutra, Guardians of the Nio Sutra, and Guardians of the Sutra of the Law.{{Cite book|和書 |title=日本の神々神徳・由来事典 | 神話と信仰にみる神祇・垂迹の姿 (わたしの家の宗教事典選書) |date=2008-03-25 |publisher=[[:en:Gakken|Gakken]] |page=224 |author=三橋 健 |isbn=978-4-05-403651-2}} The following table lists the Sanjūban-shin as recognized in Nichiren Buddhism|[http|//gyokurenzan.shinjoji.nichiren-shu.jp/introduction/sanjuban.htm {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|de|Atsuta-Schrein|de|Atsuta-Schrein|ja|玉蓮山真成寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Toyama Prefecture, Uozu City) Web site]
[https|//ryugesengyo.hatenablog.com/entry/2014/07/24/135153 List of Enshrined Deities at the Sanjūban-shin Hall of {{ill|Fukakusa Shrine|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|ja|深草山宝塔寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukakusa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fushimi Ward, Kyoto)] Furthermore, works such as {{ill|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|ja|神道大意|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Taii|1=Shintō Taii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590922}} and {{ill|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūkaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}} also list the same thirty deities.『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1913348/76 {{ill|Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|de|Shintō-Daijiten|de|Shintō-Daijiten|ja|神道大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|1=Shintō Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590924}}]』 平凡社 p.125-126（国立国会図書館） {| class="wikitable" ! state safeguarded by Buddhist teachings as recorded in various scriptures – foremost among them the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]], as well as texts such as the Vajra Transmission Sutra and [[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 毘沙門天|1=Vaiśravaṇa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q866315}}, [[:en:Dhṛtarāṣṭra|Jikokuten]], [[:en:Hariti|Kishimojin]], and [[:en:Ten Rākṣasīs|Ten Rakshasa Women]].[http|//www.hokkeshu.com/event/dic_sa_30.html 法華宗(陣門流)｜三十番神、三十一日はどうなるの] == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}} {{Shinto stub}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Medieval Shinto}} [[:en:Ja|三十番神]] [[:en:de|Dreißig Schutzgötter]] [[:en:Sn|Sanju Banshin]] [[:en:Ja|%E4%B8%89%E5%8D%81%E7%95%AA%E7%A5%9E]] {{draft categories| [[:en:Category|Shinto-Buddhism Syncretism]] [[:en:Category|Lotus Buddhism]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Deities]] [[:en:Category|Dharma Guardians]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Thirty Deities]] [[:en:Category|Completion tier 1]] [[:en:Category|11]] }} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ja:三十番神]] [[Category:Added translation template]]{{translated page|ja|三十番神|version=102345351|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 30]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Nichiren Buddhism]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] {{wikidata link|Q11354495}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11354495}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11354495]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sanjūban-shin''' (三十番神) are a group of thirty deities of the Shinto-Buddhism syncretic faith who were believed to protect the state and its people on a daily rotating basis. The Sanjūban-shin guard the thirty days of a month (since the old lunisolar calendar, [[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]], had either 29 or 30 days) in rotation. It is said that the founder of [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]], [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] (posthumously honored as Dengyō Daishi), enshrined them on [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] – this being the origin of their veneration. Their worship flourished during the Kamakura period. From the medieval era onward they were especially emphasized within [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren Buddhism]] and {{ill|Hokke Buddhism|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|ja|法華宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Buddhism|1=Hokke-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q44733349}} (in the context of {{ill|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|ja|法華神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Shintō|1=Hokke Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555328}}), being regarded as deities safeguarding the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]] – specifically as [[:en:Dharmapala|Various Benevolent Deities]]. This incorporation was undertaken by [[:en:Nichizō|Nisshō]], who, in his efforts to propagate Nichiren Buddhism in Kyoto, introduced the Mount Hiei Sanjūban-shin into his missionary work. In addition, [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shintō]] presented an alternative grouping of Sanjūban-shin – proclaiming, for instance, the “Guardians of Heaven and Earth,” “Guardians of the Royal Castle,” and “Guardians of the Nation.” [[:en:Yoshida Kanetomo|Yoshida Kanku]] argued that the Sanjūban-shin worship originated from Yoshida Shintō. In Yoshida Shintō there are also accounts linking them with the “Thirty‐two Deities” from the time of the [[:en:Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson]].{{Cite journal|和書|author=宮川了篤 |url=https|//doi.org/10.15054/00000290 |title=平成二十二年一月二十七日 最終講義 日蓮宗修法史概説 (宮川了篤先生退職記念号) |journal=身延論叢 |publisher=身延山大学仏教学会 |year=2011 |issue=16 |pages=15-16 |ISSN=1342-2715 |doi=10.15054/00000290}} 『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1224160/26 国民思想叢書. 神道篇]』　加藤咄堂 編　国民思想叢書刊行会 「神道大意」p.23-24（国立国会図書館） In 1868, with the advent of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]], the enshrinement of these deities was temporarily prohibited in the early Meiji period (although they are now freely enshrined in various forms – such as in inner sanctuary altars or subsidiary shrines – at the discretion of individual sects and temples). There are traditionally considered to be about ten different types of Sanjūban-shin, namely the Guardians of Heaven and Earth, Guardians of the Palace, Guardians of the Royal Castle, Guardians of the Nation, Guardians of the Imperial Court, Guardians of the Lotus, Guardians of the True Law, Guardians of the Lotus Sutra, Guardians of the Nio Sutra, and Guardians of the Sutra of the Law.{{Cite book|和書 |title=日本の神々神徳・由来事典 | 神話と信仰にみる神祇・垂迹の姿 (わたしの家の宗教事典選書) |date=2008-03-25 |publisher=[[:en:Gakken|Gakken]] |page=224 |author=三橋 健 |isbn=978-4-05-403651-2}} The following table lists the Sanjūban-shin as recognized in Nichiren Buddhism|[http|//gyokurenzan.shinjoji.nichiren-shu.jp/introduction/sanjuban.htm {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|de|Atsuta-Schrein|de|Atsuta-Schrein|ja|玉蓮山真成寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Toyama Prefecture, Uozu City) Web site]
[https|//ryugesengyo.hatenablog.com/entry/2014/07/24/135153 List of Enshrined Deities at the Sanjūban-shin Hall of {{ill|Fukakusa Shrine|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|ja|深草山宝塔寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukakusa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fushimi Ward, Kyoto)] Furthermore, works such as {{ill|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|ja|神道大意|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Taii|1=Shintō Taii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590922}} and {{ill|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūkaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}} also list the same thirty deities.『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1913348/76 {{ill|Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|de|Shintō-Daijiten|de|Shintō-Daijiten|ja|神道大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|1=Shintō Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590924}}]』 平凡社 p.125-126（国立国会図書館） {| class="wikitable" ! In Yoshida Shintō there are also accounts linking them with the “Thirty‐two Deities” from the time of the [[:en:Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson]].{{Cite journal|和書|author=宮川了篤 |url=https|//doi.org/10.15054/00000290 |title=平成二十二年一月二十七日 最終講義 日蓮宗修法史概説 (宮川了篤先生退職記念号) |journal=身延論叢 |publisher=身延山大学仏教学会 |year=2011 |issue=16 |pages=15-16 |ISSN=1342-2715 |doi=10.15054/00000290}} 『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1224160/26 国民思想叢書. 神道篇]』　加藤咄堂 編　国民思想叢書刊行会 「神道大意」p.23-24（国立国会図書館） In 1868, with the advent of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]], the enshrinement of these deities was temporarily prohibited in the early Meiji period (although they are now freely enshrined in various forms – such as in inner sanctuary altars or subsidiary shrines – at the discretion of individual sects and temples). There are traditionally considered to be about ten different types of Sanjūban-shin, namely the Guardians of Heaven and Earth, Guardians of the Palace, Guardians of the Royal Castle, Guardians of the Nation, Guardians of the Imperial Court, Guardians of the Lotus, Guardians of the True Law, Guardians of the Lotus Sutra, Guardians of the Nio Sutra, and Guardians of the Sutra of the Law.{{Cite book|和書 |title=日本の神々神徳・由来事典 | 神話と信仰にみる神祇・垂迹の姿 (わたしの家の宗教事典選書) |date=2008-03-25 |publisher=[[:en:Gakken|Gakken]] |page=224 |author=三橋 健 |isbn=978-4-05-403651-2}} The following table lists the Sanjūban-shin as recognized in Nichiren Buddhism|[http|//gyokurenzan.shinjoji.nichiren-shu.jp/introduction/sanjuban.htm {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|de|Atsuta-Schrein|de|Atsuta-Schrein|ja|玉蓮山真成寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Toyama Prefecture, Uozu City) Web site]
[https|//ryugesengyo.hatenablog.com/entry/2014/07/24/135153 List of Enshrined Deities at the Sanjūban-shin Hall of {{ill|Fukakusa Shrine|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|ja|深草山宝塔寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukakusa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fushimi Ward, Kyoto)] Furthermore, works such as {{ill|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|ja|神道大意|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Taii|1=Shintō Taii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590922}} and {{ill|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūkaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}} also list the same thirty deities.『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1913348/76 {{ill|Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|de|Shintō-Daijiten|de|Shintō-Daijiten|ja|神道大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|1=Shintō Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590924}}]』 平凡社 p.125-126（国立国会図書館） {| class="wikitable" ! [[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11354495]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sanjūban-shin''' (三十番神) are a group of thirty deities of the Shinto-Buddhism syncretic faith who were believed to protect the state and its people on a daily rotating basis. The Sanjūban-shin guard the thirty days of a month (since the old lunisolar calendar, [[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]], had either 29 or 30 days) in rotation. It is said that the founder of [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]], [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] (posthumously honored as Dengyō Daishi), enshrined them on [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] – this being the origin of their veneration. Their worship flourished during the Kamakura period. From the medieval era onward they were especially emphasized within [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren Buddhism]] and {{ill|Hokke Buddhism|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|ja|法華宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Buddhism|1=Hokke-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q44733349}} (in the context of {{ill|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|ja|法華神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Shintō|1=Hokke Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555328}}), being regarded as deities safeguarding the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]] – specifically as [[:en:Dharmapala|Various Benevolent Deities]]. This incorporation was undertaken by [[:en:Nichizō|Nisshō]], who, in his efforts to propagate Nichiren Buddhism in Kyoto, introduced the Mount Hiei Sanjūban-shin into his missionary work. In addition, [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shintō]] presented an alternative grouping of Sanjūban-shin – proclaiming, for instance, the “Guardians of Heaven and Earth,” “Guardians of the Royal Castle,” and “Guardians of the Nation.” [[:en:Yoshida Kanetomo|Yoshida Kanku]] argued that the Sanjūban-shin worship originated from Yoshida Shintō. In Yoshida Shintō there are also accounts linking them with the “Thirty‐two Deities” from the time of the [[:en:Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson]].{{Cite journal|和書|author=宮川了篤 |url=https|//doi.org/10.15054/00000290 |title=平成二十二年一月二十七日 最終講義 日蓮宗修法史概説 (宮川了篤先生退職記念号) |journal=身延論叢 |publisher=身延山大学仏教学会 |year=2011 |issue=16 |pages=15-16 |ISSN=1342-2715 |doi=10.15054/00000290}} 『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1224160/26 国民思想叢書. 神道篇]』　加藤咄堂 編　国民思想叢書刊行会 「神道大意」p.23-24（国立国会図書館） In 1868, with the advent of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]], the enshrinement of these deities was temporarily prohibited in the early Meiji period (although they are now freely enshrined in various forms – such as in inner sanctuary altars or subsidiary shrines – at the discretion of individual sects and temples). There are traditionally considered to be about ten different types of Sanjūban-shin, namely the Guardians of Heaven and Earth, Guardians of the Palace, Guardians of the Royal Castle, Guardians of the Nation, Guardians of the Imperial Court, Guardians of the Lotus, Guardians of the True Law, Guardians of the Lotus Sutra, Guardians of the Nio Sutra, and Guardians of the Sutra of the Law.{{Cite book|和書 |title=日本の神々神徳・由来事典 | 神話と信仰にみる神祇・垂迹の姿 (わたしの家の宗教事典選書) |date=2008-03-25 |publisher=[[:en:Gakken|Gakken]] |page=224 |author=三橋 健 |isbn=978-4-05-403651-2}} The following table lists the Sanjūban-shin as recognized in Nichiren Buddhism|[http|//gyokurenzan.shinjoji.nichiren-shu.jp/introduction/sanjuban.htm {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|de|Atsuta-Schrein|de|Atsuta-Schrein|ja|玉蓮山真成寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Toyama Prefecture, Uozu City) Web site]
[https|//ryugesengyo.hatenablog.com/entry/2014/07/24/135153 List of Enshrined Deities at the Sanjūban-shin Hall of {{ill|Fukakusa Shrine|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|ja|深草山宝塔寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukakusa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fushimi Ward, Kyoto)] Furthermore, works such as {{ill|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|ja|神道大意|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Taii|1=Shintō Taii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590922}} and {{ill|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūkaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}} also list the same thirty deities.『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1913348/76 {{ill|Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|de|Shintō-Daijiten|de|Shintō-Daijiten|ja|神道大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|1=Shintō Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590924}}]』 平凡社 p.125-126（国立国会図書館） {| class="wikitable" ! It is said that the founder of [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]], [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] (posthumously honored as Dengyō Daishi), enshrined them on [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] – this being the origin of their veneration. Their worship flourished during the Kamakura period. From the medieval era onward they were especially emphasized within [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren Buddhism]] and {{ill|Hokke Buddhism|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|ja|法華宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Buddhism|1=Hokke-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q44733349}} (in the context of {{ill|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|ja|法華神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Shintō|1=Hokke Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555328}}), being regarded as deities safeguarding the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]] – specifically as [[:en:Dharmapala|Various Benevolent Deities]]. This incorporation was undertaken by [[:en:Nichizō|Nisshō]], who, in his efforts to propagate Nichiren Buddhism in Kyoto, introduced the Mount Hiei Sanjūban-shin into his missionary work. In addition, [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shintō]] presented an alternative grouping of Sanjūban-shin – proclaiming, for instance, the “Guardians of Heaven and Earth,” “Guardians of the Royal Castle,” and “Guardians of the Nation.” [[:en:Yoshida Kanetomo|Yoshida Kanku]] argued that the Sanjūban-shin worship originated from Yoshida Shintō. In Yoshida Shintō there are also accounts linking them with the “Thirty‐two Deities” from the time of the [[:en:Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson]].{{Cite journal|和書|author=宮川了篤 |url=https|//doi.org/10.15054/00000290 |title=平成二十二年一月二十七日 最終講義 日蓮宗修法史概説 (宮川了篤先生退職記念号) |journal=身延論叢 |publisher=身延山大学仏教学会 |year=2011 |issue=16 |pages=15-16 |ISSN=1342-2715 |doi=10.15054/00000290}} 『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1224160/26 国民思想叢書. 神道篇]』　加藤咄堂 編　国民思想叢書刊行会 「神道大意」p.23-24（国立国会図書館） In 1868, with the advent of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]], the enshrinement of these deities was temporarily prohibited in the early Meiji period (although they are now freely enshrined in various forms – such as in inner sanctuary altars or subsidiary shrines – at the discretion of individual sects and temples). There are traditionally considered to be about ten different types of Sanjūban-shin, namely the Guardians of Heaven and Earth, Guardians of the Palace, Guardians of the Royal Castle, Guardians of the Nation, Guardians of the Imperial Court, Guardians of the Lotus, Guardians of the True Law, Guardians of the Lotus Sutra, Guardians of the Nio Sutra, and Guardians of the Sutra of the Law.{{Cite book|和書 |title=日本の神々神徳・由来事典 | 神話と信仰にみる神祇・垂迹の姿 (わたしの家の宗教事典選書) |date=2008-03-25 |publisher=[[:en:Gakken|Gakken]] |page=224 |author=三橋 健 |isbn=978-4-05-403651-2}} The following table lists the Sanjūban-shin as recognized in Nichiren Buddhism|[http|//gyokurenzan.shinjoji.nichiren-shu.jp/introduction/sanjuban.htm {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|de|Atsuta-Schrein|de|Atsuta-Schrein|ja|玉蓮山真成寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Toyama Prefecture, Uozu City) Web site]

Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642755}} |- |30 || Kibitsu Great Deity || [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] |} The Sanjūban-shin were established on the premise of a state safeguarded by Buddhist teachings as recorded in various scriptures – foremost among them the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]], as well as texts such as the Vajra Transmission Sutra and [[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to [[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11354495]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sanjūban-shin''' (三十番神) are a group of thirty deities of the Shinto-Buddhism syncretic faith who were believed to protect the state and its people on a daily rotating basis. The Sanjūban-shin guard the thirty days of a month (since the old lunisolar calendar, [[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]], had either 29 or 30 days) in rotation. It is said that the founder of [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]], [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] (posthumously honored as Dengyō Daishi), enshrined them on [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] – this being the origin of their veneration. Their worship flourished during the Kamakura period. From the medieval era onward they were especially emphasized within [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren Buddhism]] and {{ill|Hokke Buddhism|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|ja|法華宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Buddhism|1=Hokke-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q44733349}} (in the context of {{ill|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|ja|法華神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Shintō|1=Hokke Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555328}}), being regarded as deities safeguarding the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]] – specifically as [[:en:Dharmapala|Various Benevolent Deities]]. This incorporation was undertaken by [[:en:Nichizō|Nisshō]], who, in his efforts to propagate Nichiren Buddhism in Kyoto, introduced the Mount Hiei Sanjūban-shin into his missionary work. In addition, [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shintō]] presented an alternative grouping of Sanjūban-shin – proclaiming, for instance, the “Guardians of Heaven and Earth,” “Guardians of the Royal Castle,” and “Guardians of the Nation.” [[:en:Yoshida Kanetomo|Yoshida Kanku]] argued that the Sanjūban-shin worship originated from Yoshida Shintō. In Yoshida Shintō there are also accounts linking them with the “Thirty‐two Deities” from the time of the [[:en:Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson]].{{Cite journal|和書|author=宮川了篤 |url=https|//doi.org/10.15054/00000290 |title=平成二十二年一月二十七日 最終講義 日蓮宗修法史概説 (宮川了篤先生退職記念号) |journal=身延論叢 |publisher=身延山大学仏教学会 |year=2011 |issue=16 |pages=15-16 |ISSN=1342-2715 |doi=10.15054/00000290}} 『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1224160/26 国民思想叢書. 神道篇]』　加藤咄堂 編　国民思想叢書刊行会 「神道大意」p.23-24（国立国会図書館） In 1868, with the advent of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]], the enshrinement of these deities was temporarily prohibited in the early Meiji period (although they are now freely enshrined in various forms – such as in inner sanctuary altars or subsidiary shrines – at the discretion of individual sects and temples). There are traditionally considered to be about ten different types of Sanjūban-shin, namely the Guardians of Heaven and Earth, Guardians of the Palace, Guardians of the Royal Castle, Guardians of the Nation, Guardians of the Imperial Court, Guardians of the Lotus, Guardians of the True Law, Guardians of the Lotus Sutra, Guardians of the Nio Sutra, and Guardians of the Sutra of the Law.{{Cite book|和書 |title=日本の神々神徳・由来事典 | 神話と信仰にみる神祇・垂迹の姿 (わたしの家の宗教事典選書) |date=2008-03-25 |publisher=[[:en:Gakken|Gakken]] |page=224 |author=三橋 健 |isbn=978-4-05-403651-2}} The following table lists the Sanjūban-shin as recognized in Nichiren Buddhism|[http|//gyokurenzan.shinjoji.nichiren-shu.jp/introduction/sanjuban.htm {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|de|Atsuta-Schrein|de|Atsuta-Schrein|ja|玉蓮山真成寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Toyama Prefecture, Uozu City) Web site]

[[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 毘沙門天|1=Vaiśravaṇa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q866315}}, [[:en:Dhṛtarāṣṭra|Jikokuten]], [[:en:Hariti|Kishimojin]], and [[:en:Ten Rākṣasīs|Ten Rakshasa Women]].[http|//www.hokkeshu.com/event/dic_sa_30.html 法華宗(陣門流)｜三十番神、三十一日はどうなるの] == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}} {{Shinto stub}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Medieval Shinto}} [[:en:Ja|三十番神]] [[:en:de|Dreißig Schutzgötter]] [[:en:Sn|Sanju Banshin]] [[:en:Ja|%E4%B8%89%E5%8D%81%E7%95%AA%E7%A5%9E]] {{draft categories| [[:en:Category|Shinto-Buddhism Syncretism]] [[:en:Category|Lotus Buddhism]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Deities]] [[:en:Category|Dharma Guardians]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Thirty Deities]] [[:en:Category|Completion tier 1]] [[:en:Category|11]] }} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ja:三十番神]] [[Category:Added translation template]]{{translated page|ja|三十番神|version=102345351|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 30]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Nichiren Buddhism]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] {{wikidata link|Q11354495}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11354495}} [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

revision.text:
[[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11354495]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sanjūban-shin''' (三十番神) are a group of thirty deities of the Shinto-Buddhism syncretic faith who were believed to protect the state and its people on a daily rotating basis. The Sanjūban-shin guard the thirty days of a month (since the old lunisolar calendar, [[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]], had either 29 or 30 days) in rotation. It is said that the founder of [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]], [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] (posthumously honored as Dengyō Daishi), enshrined them on [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] – this being the origin of their veneration. Their worship flourished during the Kamakura period. From the medieval era onward they were especially emphasized within [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren Buddhism]] and {{ill|Hokke Buddhism|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|ja|法華宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Buddhism|1=Hokke-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q44733349}} (in the context of {{ill|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|ja|法華神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Shintō|1=Hokke Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555328}}), being regarded as deities safeguarding the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]] – specifically as [[:en:Dharmapala|Various Benevolent Deities]]. This incorporation was undertaken by [[:en:Nichizō|Nisshō]], who, in his efforts to propagate Nichiren Buddhism in Kyoto, introduced the Mount Hiei Sanjūban-shin into his missionary work. In addition, [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shintō]] presented an alternative grouping of Sanjūban-shin – proclaiming, for instance, the “Guardians of Heaven and Earth,” “Guardians of the Royal Castle,” and “Guardians of the Nation.” [[:en:Yoshida Kanetomo|Yoshida Kanku]] argued that the Sanjūban-shin worship originated from Yoshida Shintō. In Yoshida Shintō there are also accounts linking them with the “Thirty‐two Deities” from the time of the [[:en:Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson]].{{Cite journal|和書|author=宮川了篤 |url=https|//doi.org/10.15054/00000290 |title=平成二十二年一月二十七日 最終講義 日蓮宗修法史概説 (宮川了篤先生退職記念号) |journal=身延論叢 |publisher=身延山大学仏教学会 |year=2011 |issue=16 |pages=15-16 |ISSN=1342-2715 |doi=10.15054/00000290}} 『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1224160/26 国民思想叢書. 神道篇]』　加藤咄堂 編　国民思想叢書刊行会 「神道大意」p.23-24（国立国会図書館） In 1868, with the advent of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]], the enshrinement of these deities was temporarily prohibited in the early Meiji period (although they are now freely enshrined in various forms – such as in inner sanctuary altars or subsidiary shrines – at the discretion of individual sects and temples). There are traditionally considered to be about ten different types of Sanjūban-shin, namely the Guardians of Heaven and Earth, Guardians of the Palace, Guardians of the Royal Castle, Guardians of the Nation, Guardians of the Imperial Court, Guardians of the Lotus, Guardians of the True Law, Guardians of the Lotus Sutra, Guardians of the Nio Sutra, and Guardians of the Sutra of the Law.{{Cite book|和書 |title=日本の神々神徳・由来事典 | 神話と信仰にみる神祇・垂迹の姿 (わたしの家の宗教事典選書) |date=2008-03-25 |publisher=[[:en:Gakken|Gakken]] |page=224 |author=三橋 健 |isbn=978-4-05-403651-2}} The following table lists the Sanjūban-shin as recognized in Nichiren Buddhism|[http|//gyokurenzan.shinjoji.nichiren-shu.jp/introduction/sanjuban.htm {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|de|Atsuta-Schrein|de|Atsuta-Schrein|ja|玉蓮山真成寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Toyama Prefecture, Uozu City) Web site]
[https|//ryugesengyo.hatenablog.com/entry/2014/07/24/135153 List of Enshrined Deities at the Sanjūban-shin Hall of {{ill|Fukakusa Shrine|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|ja|深草山宝塔寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukakusa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fushimi Ward, Kyoto)] Furthermore, works such as {{ill|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|ja|神道大意|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Taii|1=Shintō Taii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590922}} and {{ill|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūkaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}} also list the same thirty deities.『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1913348/76 {{ill|Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|de|Shintō-Daijiten|de|Shintō-Daijiten|ja|神道大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|1=Shintō Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590924}}]』 平凡社 p.125-126（国立国会図書館） {| class="wikitable" ! Hakusan Hime Shrine |- |21 || {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|lt=Hachiōji Gongen|lt=Hachioji Gongen|de|Hachioji-Gongen|de|Hachioji-Gongen|ja|八王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Gongen|qq=}} || Hiyoshi Taisha Hachioji Shrine |- |22 || Inari Great Deity || [[:en:Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari Taisha]] |- |23 || Sumiyoshi Great Deity || [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] |- |24 || Gion Great Deity || [[:en:Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Shrine]] |- |25 || Akayama Great Deity || {{ill|Akayama Zen-in|de|Akayama-Zen-in|de|Akayama-Zen-in|ja|赤山禅院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akayama Zen-in|1=Sekizan Zen-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11269244}} |- |26 || Tatebe Great Deity || [[:en:Takebe taisha|Tatebe Taisha]] |- |27 || Mikami Great Deity || [[:en:Mikami title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392795}} |- |29 || Nawabuka Great Deity || {{ill|Nawabuka Shrine|de|Nawabuka-Schrein|de|Nawabuka-Schrein|ja|那波加神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawabuka Shrine|1=Nahaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642755}} |- |30 || Kibitsu Great Deity || [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] |} The Sanjūban-shin were established on the premise of a state safeguarded by Buddhist teachings as recorded in various scriptures – foremost among them the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]], as well as texts such as the Vajra Transmission Sutra and [[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 毘沙門天|1=Vaiśravaṇa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q866315}}, [[:en:Dhṛtarāṣṭra|Jikokuten]], [[:en:Hariti|Kishimojin]], and [[:en:Ten Rākṣasīs|Ten Rakshasa Women]].[http|//www.hokkeshu.com/event/dic_sa_30.html 法華宗(陣門流)｜三十番神、三十一日はどうなるの] == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}} {{Shinto stub}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Medieval Shinto}} [[:en:Ja|三十番神]] [[:en:de|Dreißig Schutzgötter]] [[:en:Sn|Sanju Banshin]] [[:en:Ja|%E4%B8%89%E5%8D%81%E7%95%AA%E7%A5%9E]] {{draft categories| [[:en:Category|Shinto-Buddhism Syncretism]] [[:en:Category|Lotus Buddhism]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Deities]] [[:en:Category|Dharma Guardians]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Thirty Deities]] [[:en:Category|Completion tier 1]] [[:en:Category|11]] }} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ja:三十番神]] [[Category:Added translation template]]{{translated page|ja|三十番神|version=102345351|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 30]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Nichiren Buddhism]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q11354495}}

Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642755}} |- |30 || Kibitsu Great Deity || [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] |} The Sanjūban-shin were established on the premise of a state safeguarded by Buddhist teachings as recorded in various scriptures – foremost among them the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]], as well as texts such as the Vajra Transmission Sutra and [[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 毘沙門天|1=Vaiśravaṇa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q866315}}, [[:en:Dhṛtarāṣṭra|Jikokuten]], [[:en:Hariti|Kishimojin]], and [[:en:Ten Rākṣasīs|Ten Rakshasa Women]].[http|//www.hokkeshu.com/event/dic_sa_30.html 法華宗(陣門流)｜三十番神、三十一日はどうなるの] == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}} {{Shinto stub}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Medieval Shinto}} [[:en:Ja|三十番神]] [[:en:de|Dreißig Schutzgötter]] [[:en:Sn|Sanju Banshin]] [[:en:Ja|%E4%B8%89%E5%8D%81%E7%95%AA%E7%A5%9E]] {{draft categories| [[:en:Category|Shinto-Buddhism Syncretism]] [[:en:Category|Lotus Buddhism]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Deities]] [[:en:Category|Dharma Guardians]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Thirty Deities]] [[:en:Category|Completion tier 1]] [[:en:Category|11]] }} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ja:三十番神]] [[Category:Added translation template]]{{translated page|ja|三十番神|version=102345351|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 30]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Nichiren Buddhism]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11354495}}

[[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11354495]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sanjūban-shin''' (三十番神) are a group of thirty deities of the Shinto-Buddhism syncretic faith who were believed to protect the state and its people on a daily rotating basis. The Sanjūban-shin guard the thirty days of a month (since the old lunisolar calendar, [[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]], had either 29 or 30 days) in rotation. It is said that the founder of [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]], [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] (posthumously honored as Dengyō Daishi), enshrined them on [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] – this being the origin of their veneration. Their worship flourished during the Kamakura period. From the medieval era onward they were especially emphasized within [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren Buddhism]] and {{ill|Hokke Buddhism|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|ja|法華宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Buddhism|1=Hokke-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q44733349}} (in the context of {{ill|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|ja|法華神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Shintō|1=Hokke Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555328}}), being regarded as deities safeguarding the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]] – specifically as [[:en:Dharmapala|Various Benevolent Deities]]. This incorporation was undertaken by [[:en:Nichizō|Nisshō]], who, in his efforts to propagate Nichiren Buddhism in Kyoto, introduced the Mount Hiei Sanjūban-shin into his missionary work. In addition, [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shintō]] presented an alternative grouping of Sanjūban-shin – proclaiming, for instance, the “Guardians of Heaven and Earth,” “Guardians of the Royal Castle,” and “Guardians of the Nation.” [[:en:Yoshida Kanetomo|Yoshida Kanku]] argued that the Sanjūban-shin worship originated from Yoshida Shintō. In Yoshida Shintō there are also accounts linking them with the “Thirty‐two Deities” from the time of the [[:en:Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson]].{{Cite journal|和書|author=宮川了篤 |url=https|//doi.org/10.15054/00000290 |title=平成二十二年一月二十七日 最終講義 日蓮宗修法史概説 (宮川了篤先生退職記念号) |journal=身延論叢 |publisher=身延山大学仏教学会 |year=2011 |issue=16 |pages=15-16 |ISSN=1342-2715 |doi=10.15054/00000290}} 『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1224160/26 国民思想叢書. 神道篇]』　加藤咄堂 編　国民思想叢書刊行会 「神道大意」p.23-24（国立国会図書館） In 1868, with the advent of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]], the enshrinement of these deities was temporarily prohibited in the early Meiji period (although they are now freely enshrined in various forms – such as in inner sanctuary altars or subsidiary shrines – at the discretion of individual sects and temples). There are traditionally considered to be about ten different types of Sanjūban-shin, namely the Guardians of Heaven and Earth, Guardians of the Palace, Guardians of the Royal Castle, Guardians of the Nation, Guardians of the Imperial Court, Guardians of the Lotus, Guardians of the True Law, Guardians of the Lotus Sutra, Guardians of the Nio Sutra, and Guardians of the Sutra of the Law.{{Cite book|和書 |title=日本の神々神徳・由来事典 | 神話と信仰にみる神祇・垂迹の姿 (わたしの家の宗教事典選書) |date=2008-03-25 |publisher=[[:en:Gakken|Gakken]] |page=224 |author=三橋 健 |isbn=978-4-05-403651-2}} The following table lists the Sanjūban-shin as recognized in Nichiren Buddhism|[http|//gyokurenzan.shinjoji.nichiren-shu.jp/introduction/sanjuban.htm {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|de|Atsuta-Schrein|de|Atsuta-Schrein|ja|玉蓮山真成寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Toyama Prefecture, Uozu City) Web site]
[https|//ryugesengyo.hatenablog.com/entry/2014/07/24/135153 List of Enshrined Deities at the Sanjūban-shin Hall of {{ill|Fukakusa Shrine|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|ja|深草山宝塔寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukakusa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fushimi Ward, Kyoto)] Furthermore, works such as {{ill|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|ja|神道大意|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Taii|1=Shintō Taii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590922}} and {{ill|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūkaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}} also list the same thirty deities.『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1913348/76 {{ill|Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|de|Shintō-Daijiten|de|Shintō-Daijiten|ja|神道大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|1=Shintō Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590924}}]』 平凡社 p.125-126（国立国会図書館） {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine|Mikami Shrine]] |- |28 || Hyoshuda Great Deity || {{ill|Hyoshuda Taisha|de|Hyoshuda-Taisha|de|Hyoshuda-Taisha|ja|兵主大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyoshuda Taisha|1=Hyozu-taisha|comment=changed Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642755}} |- |30 || Kibitsu Great Deity || [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] |} The Sanjūban-shin were established on the premise of a state safeguarded by Buddhist teachings as recorded in various scriptures – foremost among them the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]], as well as texts such as the Vajra Transmission Sutra and [[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 毘沙門天|1=Vaiśravaṇa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q866315}}, [[:en:Dhṛtarāṣṭra|Jikokuten]], [[:en:Hariti|Kishimojin]], and [[:en:Ten Rākṣasīs|Ten Rakshasa Women]].[http|//www.hokkeshu.com/event/dic_sa_30.html 法華宗(陣門流)｜三十番神、三十一日はどうなるの] == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}} {{Shinto stub}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Medieval Shinto}} [[:en:Ja|三十番神]] [[:en:de|Dreißig Schutzgötter]] [[:en:Sn|Sanju Banshin]] [[:en:Ja|%E4%B8%89%E5%8D%81%E7%95%AA%E7%A5%9E]] {{draft categories| [[:en:Category|Shinto-Buddhism Syncretism]] [[:en:Category|Lotus Buddhism]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Deities]] [[:en:Category|Dharma Guardians]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Thirty Deities]] [[:en:Category|Completion tier 1]] [[:en:Category|11]] }} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ja:三十番神]] [[Category:Added translation template]]{{translated page|ja|三十番神|version=102345351|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 30]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Nichiren Buddhism]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11354495}}

[[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] [[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] [[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11354495]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sanjūban-shin''' (三十番神) are a group of thirty deities of the Shinto-Buddhism syncretic faith who were believed to protect the state and its people on a daily rotating basis. The Sanjūban-shin guard the thirty days of a month (since the old lunisolar calendar, [[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]], had either 29 or 30 days) in rotation. It is said that the founder of [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]], [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] (posthumously honored as Dengyō Daishi), enshrined them on [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] – this being the origin of their veneration. Their worship flourished during the Kamakura period. From the medieval era onward they were especially emphasized within [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren Buddhism]] and {{ill|Hokke Buddhism|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|ja|法華宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Buddhism|1=Hokke-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q44733349}} (in the context of {{ill|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|ja|法華神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Shintō|1=Hokke Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555328}}), being regarded as deities safeguarding the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]] – specifically as [[:en:Dharmapala|Various Benevolent Deities]]. This incorporation was undertaken by [[:en:Nichizō|Nisshō]], who, in his efforts to propagate Nichiren Buddhism in Kyoto, introduced the Mount Hiei Sanjūban-shin into his missionary work. In addition, [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shintō]] presented an alternative grouping of Sanjūban-shin – proclaiming, for instance, the “Guardians of Heaven and Earth,” “Guardians of the Royal Castle,” and “Guardians of the Nation.” [[:en:Yoshida Kanetomo|Yoshida Kanku]] argued that the Sanjūban-shin worship originated from Yoshida Shintō. In Yoshida Shintō there are also accounts linking them with the “Thirty‐two Deities” from the time of the [[:en:Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson]].{{Cite journal|和書|author=宮川了篤 |url=https|//doi.org/10.15054/00000290 |title=平成二十二年一月二十七日 最終講義 日蓮宗修法史概説 (宮川了篤先生退職記念号) |journal=身延論叢 |publisher=身延山大学仏教学会 |year=2011 |issue=16 |pages=15-16 |ISSN=1342-2715 |doi=10.15054/00000290}} 『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1224160/26 国民思想叢書. 神道篇]』　加藤咄堂 編　国民思想叢書刊行会 「神道大意」p.23-24（国立国会図書館） In 1868, with the advent of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]], the enshrinement of these deities was temporarily prohibited in the early Meiji period (although they are now freely enshrined in various forms – such as in inner sanctuary altars or subsidiary shrines – at the discretion of individual sects and temples). There are traditionally considered to be about ten different types of Sanjūban-shin, namely the Guardians of Heaven and Earth, Guardians of the Palace, Guardians of the Royal Castle, Guardians of the Nation, Guardians of the Imperial Court, Guardians of the Lotus, Guardians of the True Law, Guardians of the Lotus Sutra, Guardians of the Nio Sutra, and Guardians of the Sutra of the Law.{{Cite book|和書 |title=日本の神々神徳・由来事典 | 神話と信仰にみる神祇・垂迹の姿 (わたしの家の宗教事典選書) |date=2008-03-25 |publisher=[[:en:Gakken|Gakken]] |page=224 |author=三橋 健 |isbn=978-4-05-403651-2}} The following table lists the Sanjūban-shin as recognized in Nichiren Buddhism|[http|//gyokurenzan.shinjoji.nichiren-shu.jp/introduction/sanjuban.htm {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|de|Atsuta-Schrein|de|Atsuta-Schrein|ja|玉蓮山真成寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Toyama Prefecture, Uozu City) Web site]
[https|//ryugesengyo.hatenablog.com/entry/2014/07/24/135153 List of Enshrined Deities at the Sanjūban-shin Hall of {{ill|Fukakusa Shrine|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|ja|深草山宝塔寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukakusa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fushimi Ward, Kyoto)] Furthermore, works such as {{ill|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|ja|神道大意|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Taii|1=Shintō Taii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590922}} and {{ill|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūkaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}} also list the same thirty deities.『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1913348/76 {{ill|Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|de|Shintō-Daijiten|de|Shintō-Daijiten|ja|神道大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|1=Shintō Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590924}}]』 平凡社 p.125-126（国立国会図書館） {| class="wikitable" ! [[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] [[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] [[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11354495]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sanjūban-shin''' (三十番神) are a group of thirty deities of the Shinto-Buddhism syncretic faith who were believed to protect the state and its people on a daily rotating basis. The Sanjūban-shin guard the thirty days of a month (since the old lunisolar calendar, [[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]], had either 29 or 30 days) in rotation. It is said that the founder of [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]], [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] (posthumously honored as Dengyō Daishi), enshrined them on [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] – this being the origin of their veneration. Their worship flourished during the Kamakura period. From the medieval era onward they were especially emphasized within [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren Buddhism]] and {{ill|Hokke Buddhism|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|ja|法華宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Buddhism|1=Hokke-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q44733349}} (in the context of {{ill|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|ja|法華神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Shintō|1=Hokke Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555328}}), being regarded as deities safeguarding the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]] – specifically as [[:en:Dharmapala|Various Benevolent Deities]]. This incorporation was undertaken by [[:en:Nichizō|Nisshō]], who, in his efforts to propagate Nichiren Buddhism in Kyoto, introduced the Mount Hiei Sanjūban-shin into his missionary work. In addition, [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shintō]] presented an alternative grouping of Sanjūban-shin – proclaiming, for instance, the “Guardians of Heaven and Earth,” “Guardians of the Royal Castle,” and “Guardians of the Nation.” [[:en:Yoshida Kanetomo|Yoshida Kanku]] argued that the Sanjūban-shin worship originated from Yoshida Shintō. state safeguarded by Buddhist teachings as recorded in various scriptures – foremost among them the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]], as well as texts such as the Vajra Transmission Sutra and [[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 毘沙門天|1=Vaiśravaṇa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q866315}}, [[:en:Dhṛtarāṣṭra|Jikokuten]], [[:en:Hariti|Kishimojin]], and [[:en:Ten Rākṣasīs|Ten Rakshasa Women]].[http|//www.hokkeshu.com/event/dic_sa_30.html 法華宗(陣門流)｜三十番神、三十一日はどうなるの] == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}} {{Shinto stub}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Medieval Shinto}} [[:en:Ja|三十番神]] [[:en:de|Dreißig Schutzgötter]] [[:en:Sn|Sanju Banshin]] [[:en:Ja|%E4%B8%89%E5%8D%81%E7%95%AA%E7%A5%9E]] {{draft categories| [[:en:Category|Shinto-Buddhism Syncretism]] [[:en:Category|Lotus Buddhism]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Deities]] [[:en:Category|Dharma Guardians]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Thirty Deities]] [[:en:Category|Completion tier 1]] [[:en:Category|11]] }} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ja:三十番神]] [[Category:Added translation template]]{{translated page|ja|三十番神|version=102345351|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 30]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Nichiren Buddhism]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11354495}} [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

[[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] [[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] [[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11354495]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sanjūban-shin''' (三十番神) are a group of thirty deities of the Shinto-Buddhism syncretic faith who were believed to protect the state and its people on a daily rotating basis. The Sanjūban-shin guard the thirty days of a month (since the old lunisolar calendar, [[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]], had either 29 or 30 days) in rotation. It is said that the founder of [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]], [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] (posthumously honored as Dengyō Daishi), enshrined them on [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] – this being the origin of their veneration. Their worship flourished during the Kamakura period. From the medieval era onward they were especially emphasized within [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren Buddhism]] and {{ill|Hokke Buddhism|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|ja|法華宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Buddhism|1=Hokke-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q44733349}} (in the context of {{ill|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|ja|法華神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Shintō|1=Hokke Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555328}}), being regarded as deities safeguarding the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]] – specifically as [[:en:Dharmapala|Various Benevolent Deities]]. This incorporation was undertaken by [[:en:Nichizō|Nisshō]], who, in his efforts to propagate Nichiren Buddhism in Kyoto, introduced the Mount Hiei Sanjūban-shin into his missionary work. In addition, [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shintō]] presented an alternative grouping of Sanjūban-shin – proclaiming, for instance, the “Guardians of Heaven and Earth,” “Guardians of the Royal Castle,” and “Guardians of the Nation.” [[:en:Yoshida Kanetomo|Yoshida Kanku]] argued that the Sanjūban-shin worship originated from Yoshida Shintō. In Yoshida Shintō there are also accounts linking them with the “Thirty‐two Deities” from the time of the [[:en:Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson]].{{Cite journal|和書|author=宮川了篤 |url=https|//doi.org/10.15054/00000290 |title=平成二十二年一月二十七日 最終講義 日蓮宗修法史概説 (宮川了篤先生退職記念号) |journal=身延論叢 |publisher=身延山大学仏教学会 |year=2011 |issue=16 |pages=15-16 |ISSN=1342-2715 |doi=10.15054/00000290}} 『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1224160/26 国民思想叢書. 神道篇]』　加藤咄堂 編　国民思想叢書刊行会 「神道大意」p.23-24（国立国会図書館） In 1868, with the advent of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]], the enshrinement of these deities was temporarily prohibited in the early Meiji period (although they are now freely enshrined in various forms – such as in inner sanctuary altars or subsidiary shrines – at the discretion of individual sects and temples). There are traditionally considered to be about ten different types of Sanjūban-shin, namely the Guardians of Heaven and Earth, Guardians of the Palace, Guardians of the Royal Castle, Guardians of the Nation, Guardians of the Imperial Court, Guardians of the Lotus, Guardians of the True Law, Guardians of the Lotus Sutra, Guardians of the Nio Sutra, and Guardians of the Sutra of the Law.{{Cite book|和書 |title=日本の神々神徳・由来事典 | 神話と信仰にみる神祇・垂迹の姿 (わたしの家の宗教事典選書) |date=2008-03-25 |publisher=[[:en:Gakken|Gakken]] |page=224 |author=三橋 健 |isbn=978-4-05-403651-2}} The following table lists the Sanjūban-shin as recognized in Nichiren Buddhism|[http|//gyokurenzan.shinjoji.nichiren-shu.jp/introduction/sanjuban.htm {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|de|Atsuta-Schrein|de|Atsuta-Schrein|ja|玉蓮山真成寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Toyama Prefecture, Uozu City) Web site]
[https|//ryugesengyo.hatenablog.com/entry/2014/07/24/135153 List of Enshrined Deities at the Sanjūban-shin Hall of {{ill|Fukakusa Shrine|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|ja|深草山宝塔寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukakusa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fushimi Ward, Kyoto)] Furthermore, works such as {{ill|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|ja|神道大意|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Taii|1=Shintō Taii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590922}} and {{ill|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūkaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}} also list the same thirty deities.『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1913348/76 {{ill|Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|de|Shintō-Daijiten|de|Shintō-Daijiten|ja|神道大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|1=Shintō Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590924}}]』 平凡社 p.125-126（国立国会図書館） {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642755}} |- |30 || Kibitsu Great Deity || [[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] |} The Sanjūban-shin were established on the premise of a state safeguarded by Buddhist teachings as recorded in various scriptures – foremost among them the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]], as well as texts such as the Vajra Transmission Sutra and [[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 毘沙門天|1=Vaiśravaṇa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q866315}}, [[:en:Dhṛtarāṣṭra|Jikokuten]], [[:en:Hariti|Kishimojin]], and [[:en:Ten Rākṣasīs|Ten Rakshasa Women]].[http|//www.hokkeshu.com/event/dic_sa_30.html 法華宗(陣門流)｜三十番神、三十一日はどうなるの] == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}} {{Shinto stub}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Medieval Shinto}} [[:en:Ja|三十番神]] [[:en:de|Dreißig Schutzgötter]] [[:en:Sn|Sanju Banshin]] [[:en:Ja|%E4%B8%89%E5%8D%81%E7%95%AA%E7%A5%9E]] {{draft categories| [[:en:Category|Shinto-Buddhism Syncretism]] [[:en:Category|Lotus Buddhism]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Deities]] [[:en:Category|Dharma Guardians]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Thirty Deities]] [[:en:Category|Completion tier 1]] [[:en:Category|11]] }} [[ja:三十番神]] {{translated page|ja|三十番神|version=102345351|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q11354495}} [[Category:Added translation template]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 30]] [[Category:Nichiren Buddhism]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

state safeguarded by Buddhist teachings as recorded in various scriptures – foremost among them the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]], as well as texts such as the Vajra Transmission Sutra and [[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 毘沙門天|1=Vaiśravaṇa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q866315}}, [[:en:Dhṛtarāṣṭra|Jikokuten]], [[:en:Hariti|Kishimojin]], and [[:en:Ten Rākṣasīs|Ten Rakshasa Women]].[http|//www.hokkeshu.com/event/dic_sa_30.html 法華宗(陣門流)｜三十番神、三十一日はどうなるの] == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}} {{Shinto stub}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Medieval Shinto}} [[:en:Ja|三十番神]] [[:en:de|Dreißig Schutzgötter]] [[:en:Sn|Sanju Banshin]] [[:en:Ja|%E4%B8%89%E5%8D%81%E7%95%AA%E7%A5%9E]] {{draft categories| [[:en:Category|Shinto-Buddhism Syncretism]] [[:en:Category|Lotus Buddhism]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Deities]] [[:en:Category|Dharma Guardians]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Thirty Deities]] [[:en:Category|Completion tier 1]] [[:en:Category|11]] }} [[ja:三十番神]] {{translated page|ja|三十番神|version=102345351|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q11354495}} [[Category:Added translation template]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 30]] [[Category:Nichiren Buddhism]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] [[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] [[en:Sanjūbanshin]][[ja:三十番神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11354495]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sanjūban-shin''' (三十番神) are a group of thirty deities of the Shinto-Buddhism syncretic faith who were believed to protect the state and its people on a daily rotating basis. The Sanjūban-shin guard the thirty days of a month (since the old lunisolar calendar, [[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]], had either 29 or 30 days) in rotation. It is said that the founder of [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai Buddhism]], [[:en:Saichō|Saichō]] (posthumously honored as Dengyō Daishi), enshrined them on [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] – this being the origin of their veneration. Their worship flourished during the Kamakura period. From the medieval era onward they were especially emphasized within [[:en:Nichiren-shū|Nichiren Buddhism]] and {{ill|Hokke Buddhism|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|de|Hokke-Buddhismus|ja|法華宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Buddhism|1=Hokke-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q44733349}} (in the context of {{ill|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|de|Hokke Shintō|ja|法華神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokke Shintō|1=Hokke Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555328}}), being regarded as deities safeguarding the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]] – specifically as [[:en:Dharmapala|Various Benevolent Deities]]. This incorporation was undertaken by [[:en:Nichizō|Nisshō]], who, in his efforts to propagate Nichiren Buddhism in Kyoto, introduced the Mount Hiei Sanjūban-shin into his missionary work. In addition, [[:en:Yoshida Shintō|Yoshida Shintō]] presented an alternative grouping of Sanjūban-shin – proclaiming, for instance, the “Guardians of Heaven and Earth,” “Guardians of the Royal Castle,” and “Guardians of the Nation.” [[:en:Yoshida Kanetomo|Yoshida Kanku]] argued that the Sanjūban-shin worship originated from Yoshida Shintō. In Yoshida Shintō there are also accounts linking them with the “Thirty‐two Deities” from the time of the [[:en:Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Heavenly Grandson]].{{Cite journal|和書|author=宮川了篤 |url=https|//doi.org/10.15054/00000290 |title=平成二十二年一月二十七日 最終講義 日蓮宗修法史概説 (宮川了篤先生退職記念号) |journal=身延論叢 |publisher=身延山大学仏教学会 |year=2011 |issue=16 |pages=15-16 |ISSN=1342-2715 |doi=10.15054/00000290}} 『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1224160/26 国民思想叢書. 神道篇]』　加藤咄堂 編　国民思想叢書刊行会 「神道大意」p.23-24（国立国会図書館） In 1868, with the advent of [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]], the enshrinement of these deities was temporarily prohibited in the early Meiji period (although they are now freely enshrined in various forms – such as in inner sanctuary altars or subsidiary shrines – at the discretion of individual sects and temples). There are traditionally considered to be about ten different types of Sanjūban-shin, namely the Guardians of Heaven and Earth, Guardians of the Palace, Guardians of the Royal Castle, Guardians of the Nation, Guardians of the Imperial Court, Guardians of the Lotus, Guardians of the True Law, Guardians of the Lotus Sutra, Guardians of the Nio Sutra, and Guardians of the Sutra of the Law.{{Cite book|和書 |title=日本の神々神徳・由来事典 | 神話と信仰にみる神祇・垂迹の姿 (わたしの家の宗教事典選書) |date=2008-03-25 |publisher=[[:en:Gakken|Gakken]] |page=224 |author=三橋 健 |isbn=978-4-05-403651-2}} The following table lists the Sanjūban-shin as recognized in Nichiren Buddhism|[http|//gyokurenzan.shinjoji.nichiren-shu.jp/introduction/sanjuban.htm {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|de|Atsuta-Schrein|de|Atsuta-Schrein|ja|玉蓮山真成寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuta Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Toyama Prefecture, Uozu City) Web site]
[https|//ryugesengyo.hatenablog.com/entry/2014/07/24/135153 List of Enshrined Deities at the Sanjūban-shin Hall of {{ill|Fukakusa Shrine|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|de|Fukakusa-Schrein|ja|深草山宝塔寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukakusa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Fushimi Ward, Kyoto)] Furthermore, works such as {{ill|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|de|Shintō Taii|ja|神道大意|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Taii|1=Shintō Taii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590922}} and {{ill|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|de|Shūkaishō|ja|拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūkaishō|1=Shūgaishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11497878}} also list the same thirty deities.『[https|//dl.ndl.go.jp/info|ndljp/pid/1913348/76 {{ill|Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|de|Shintō-Daijiten|de|Shintō-Daijiten|ja|神道大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintō Daijiten, Vol.2|1=Shintō Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590924}}]』 平凡社 p.125-126（国立国会図書館） {| class="wikitable" ! state safeguarded by Buddhist teachings as recorded in various scriptures – foremost among them the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]], as well as texts such as the Vajra Transmission Sutra and [[:en:Humane King Sutra|Nio Prajñāpāramitā Sutra]]. In the course of the development of Shinto-Buddhism syncretism, these deities were incorporated from the native Japanese pantheon. In the modern era, because the adoption of the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Solar Calendar]] results in a 31st day each month, on the 31st day some traditions invoke a group known as the Five Benevolent Deities – namely [[:en:Bhaiṣajyarāja|Yakushi Bodhisattva]], Yūshi Bodhisattva, {{ill|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|de|Tamonten|ja|多聞天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 毘沙門天|1=Vaiśravaṇa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q866315}}, [[:en:Dhṛtarāṣṭra|Jikokuten]], [[:en:Hariti|Kishimojin]], and [[:en:Ten Rākṣasīs|Ten Rakshasa Women]].[http|//www.hokkeshu.com/event/dic_sa_30.html 法華宗(陣門流)｜三十番神、三十一日はどうなるの] == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|de|Liste der Buddhas|ja|仏の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の仏尊|1=Japanese Buddhist pantheon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6158131}} {{Shinto stub}} {{Medieval Shinto}} {{Medieval Shinto}} [[:en:Ja|三十番神]] [[:en:de|Dreißig Schutzgötter]] [[:en:Sn|Sanju Banshin]] [[:en:Ja|%E4%B8%89%E5%8D%81%E7%95%AA%E7%A5%9E]] {{draft categories| [[:en:Category|Shinto-Buddhism Syncretism]] [[:en:Category|Lotus Buddhism]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Deities]] [[:en:Category|Dharma Guardians]] [[:en:Category|Japanese Thirty Deities]] [[:en:Category|Completion tier 1]] [[:en:Category|11]] }} [[ja:三十番神]] {{translated page|ja|三十番神|version=102345351|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q11354495}} [[Category:Added translation template]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 30]] [[Category:Nichiren Buddhism]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
